teamObjective.explanation: As the opening leader with a very weak hand (3-6 of spades, no trumps, no bombs), the immediate team objective is to start shedding cards cheaply while preserving whatever little high-card equity I have. Leading the lowest card maximizes the chance the trick passes around and I retain the lead, letting me continue to unload my remaining low singles.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) has no public actions to interpret yet. Their hand count is 3, equal to each opponent. I believe they would prefer a low, controllable lead that does not force them to spend a trump or high card unless they choose to. They cannot signal anything at this stage.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents (players 1 and 3) each hold 3 unknown cards. Since trump rank is 7, any 7 or higher non-trump, any trump, or any bomb can beat my highest card. It is highly likely at least one opponent can beat any single I lead, so I should not expect to win the trick with a high card. Their rational aim is to seize control with the smallest card that beats mine.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of spades is the minimal-cost opener: it sheds my weakest card, keeps the 4-5-6 sequence intact, and gives me the best chance that all opponents pass and I keep the lead. If an opponent does take the trick, they have spent a higher card for a low gain.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Leading the 4 uses a slightly stronger card but does not change the fact that opponents can easily beat it; it also weakens my follow-up sequence and reduces my chance of retaining the lead.
The 5 is a middle card with no special controlling power; spending it first leaves me with even weaker follow-ups and does not improve my chance of winning the trick.

The 6 is my highest non-trump and my only realistic chance to win a low single trick. Expending it on an opening lead that will almost certainly be trumped or overcut is wasteful and leaves me with only 3-4-5.

risks: An opponent may play a 7 or higher / trump to take the lead immediately.
Partner may not be able to help if the lead gets contested.
Leading low reveals I have low spades, though this is unavoidable.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the absolute lowest card, I minimize the value surrendered if the trick is lost and maximize the probability of a pass-around that lets me continue leading my remaining cards.

notes: Opening lead with no public history and a weak, single-suited low hand; lowest-card lead is the standard safe play.
